text: The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to select a distal margin and split the omentum at the mid-transverse colon. The mesentery is dissected close to the bowel wall, with the surgeon approaching from both sides and meeting in the middle at the terminal ileum mesentery.

The transverse colon is visibly looped, and the surgeon returns to the ascending colon site. The duodenum is carefully swept down from the back of the mesentery, leaving only the lateral attachments remaining after the mesentery has been taken.

The surgeon divides the margins and proceeds to take down the adherence of the right colon. A perforation into the anterior abdominal wall is revealed, which was not visible on the recent CAT scan. The bowel appears liquefied from a previous ischemic event. The surgeon removes the remaining lateral attachments, including those adhering the colon to the liver, and separates the omentum from the liver.

The surgeon identifies the area of perforation and prepares for an isoperistaltic ureteral anastomosis. Using a stapler through the single incision at the 12mm port, the surgeon creates the common channel with a white load stapler to minimize bleeding risk. The common channel is then sewn closed, with the surgeon inspecting the colon's interior and noting the healthy pink mucosa without signs of ischemia. The suturing begins at the near corner and progresses outward to the far corner.
